text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Gynecologic Oncology
SERVICE: Gynecologic Oncology Consultation
REASON FOR CONSULTATION: New diagnosis of advanced ovarian cancer, surgical planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old woman of West African (Ghanaian) descent who presents to the gynecologic oncology clinic for initial consultation following a new diagnosis of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, with peritoneal carcinomatosis. She is referred by her gynecologist after diagnostic laparoscopy and omental biopsy confirmed the diagnosis. She presents today with her husband to discuss surgical planning and overall management.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports a 4-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and a 6 kg (approximately 13 lb) unintentional weight loss. She initially attributed the bloating and fullness to dietary changes and work-related stress, and delayed seeking medical attention for several weeks. Over the past 6-8 weeks, she has noticed increasing bilateral lower pelvic pressure and a sensation of heaviness in the lower abdomen. She also reports urinary frequency without dysuria, urgency, or hematuria, which she attributes to external pressure from a pelvic mass. On further questioning, she describes fatigue that has progressively worsened over the same time course, now limiting her ability to sustain a full workday without rest periods. She denies nausea, vomiting, melena, hematochezia, or any changes in bowel habits. She denies hemoptysis, cough, or chest pain.

Her symptom onset prompted referral from her primary care physician to her gynecologist after a pelvic ultrasound demonstrated bilateral complex adnexal masses. Subsequent CA-125 was markedly elevated at 3,847 U/mL and HE4 was elevated at 412 pmol/L, both strongly suggestive of epithelial ovarian malignancy. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated bilateral complex adnexal masses (right 7.2 x 6.1 x 5.8 cm, left 5.8 x 4.3 cm), moderate-volume ascites, omental caking with confluent soft tissue density measuring up to 11 cm in maximum dimension, multiple peritoneal implants along the right paracolic gutter, left pelvic peritoneum, and small bowel mesentery (largest 2.3 cm), and bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy (right external iliac 1.6 cm, left para-aortic 1.8 cm). No pleural effusion, pulmonary nodules, or parenchymal liver metastases were identified. The CT impression noted disease appearing surgically resectable without suprarenal or supradiaphragmatic involvement, with an estimated Peritoneal Cancer Index of approximately 18.

Diagnostic laparoscopy and omental biopsy were subsequently performed. Pathology from the omental biopsy confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma of Mullerian origin: sheets and papillary clusters of highly atypical epithelial cells with prominent nucleoli, marked nuclear pleomorphism, and abundant mitoses including atypical forms; psammoma bodies present; immunohistochemical profile showing WT-1 positive (diffuse), PAX8 positive (diffuse), p53 aberrant (diffuse strong overexpression consistent with TP53 gain-of-function mutation), ER weakly positive, CK7 positive, CK20 negative, and CA-125 positive; Ki-67 proliferation index 78%. Peritoneal washings were positive for malignant cells with serous features.

Germline genetic testing has also been completed and results have been reviewed with the patient: she was found to carry a pathogenic germline BRCA1 variant c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74, the 5382insC founder mutation), classified as pathogenic. Tumor NGS (Foundation One CDx on the omental biopsy specimen) demonstrated somatic TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68%), BRCA1 LOH at the germline locus (VAF 82%, consistent with loss of heterozygosity), RB1 deep deletion, CCNE1 amplification, and subclonal NF1 and PIK3CA alterations. HRD score was 68 (positive) with genomic LOH of 31%. Tumor mutational burden was low (4.2 mut/Mb) and microsatellite status was stable. The germline BRCA1 variant with somatic LOH is highly predictive of PARP inhibitor sensitivity, which has important implications for maintenance therapy planning following chemotherapy.

The patient has been informed of her diagnosis and understands its gravity. She is emotionally distressed, particularly regarding her prognosis and the implications of the BRCA1 finding for her daughter and sister. She and her husband have many questions today. She was referred to oncology social work prior to this visit.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: Abdominal distension (progressive over 4 months), bilateral lower pelvic pressure and heaviness, urinary frequency, early satiety, 6 kg unintentional weight loss over 4 months, fatigue (Grade 2 per CTCAE, limiting some activities), mild dyspnea on exertion (attributed to elevated diaphragm from ascites).
Negative: No fevers or chills, no night sweats, no nausea or vomiting, no change in bowel habits, no melena or hematochezia, no hemoptysis, no chest pain, no dysuria or hematuria, no lower extremity edema, no headaches, no bone pain.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. No prior malignancy.
2. No prior abdominal or pelvic surgery.
3. Hypertension — mild, previously well-controlled on no medications (managed with lifestyle modification per patient and PCP records).
4. Iron deficiency anemia — intermittent, managed with dietary modification; no recent supplementation.
5. No history of venous thromboembolism.
6. No history of cardiac disease.
7. No history of diabetes mellitus.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Diagnostic laparoscopy and omental biopsy (recent, for current illness) — uncomplicated.
- No other prior surgeries.

OBSTETRIC AND GYNECOLOGIC HISTORY:
- G2P2; two vaginal deliveries without complications.
- Menopause: premenopausal at time of presentation; last menstrual period approximately 6 months prior (menstrual irregularity noted in the preceding year, attributed to perimenopause by her gynecologist).
- No history of abnormal Pap smears or cervical dysplasia.
- No prior use of oral contraceptives (patient never used OCP; this is relevant as OCP use is associated with reduced ovarian cancer risk in BRCA carriers).
- No prior hormone replacement therapy.
- No history of endometriosis.

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Maternal aunt: breast cancer, diagnosed age 38 (bilateral; survived).
- Maternal grandmother: ovarian cancer, diagnosed age 55 (deceased, cause of death ovarian cancer).
- Mother: no known malignancy; alive.
- Father: hypertension, no malignancy.
- Two children: daughter age 22, son age 18; no known health issues.
- No known male relatives with breast cancer or pancreatic cancer (which can be associated with BRCA1/2 pathogenic variants).
- Family history pattern strongly suggestive of hereditary breast-ovarian cancer syndrome, consistent with confirmed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant. Patient was referred for genetic counseling several months ago but testing was completed only recently in the context of the current diagnosis. Genetic counseling was conducted pre-biopsy; results subsequently disclosed as above.

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Born in Accra, Ghana; emigrated to the United States at age 22.
- Married, lives with husband and youngest child (son, age 18).
- Works as a secondary school mathematics teacher; currently on medical leave pending surgery.
- Nonsmoker (never smoked).
- Alcohol: rare social use, less than 1 drink per week; denies binge drinking.
- Recreational drugs: none.
- No known occupational exposures.
- Religious: Christian; attends church regularly; faith is an important source of support.
- Health insurance: through employer (public school system); coverage confirmed for surgical and oncologic care.
- Support system: husband very engaged; has a close network of family and church community. Referred to oncology social work for additional psychosocial support; patient also expressed interest in connecting with a gynecologic cancer support group.

ALLERGIES:
- No known drug allergies.
- No known food allergies.
- No prior chemotherapy exposure; platinum allergy risk not yet known.

MEDICATIONS:
1. Multivitamin — one tablet orally daily.
2. Ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally once daily (recently started by PCP for iron deficiency anemia).
3. No prescription medications currently; mild hypertension managed with lifestyle modification.
4. No anticoagulants, antiplatelet agents, or NSAIDs.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Alert and oriented woman in no acute distress; cooperative and tearful at times during the visit. Well-nourished but appears mildly cachectic. Wearing a colorful headwrap.
Vital signs: Temperature 37.1°C, blood pressure 128/82 mmHg, heart rate 84 bpm, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight 64 kg, height 163 cm, BSA 1.74 m2.
Performance status: ECOG 1 (ambulatory, able to carry out light work but restricted in strenuous activity).
Head and neck: No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy palpated. No thyroid enlargement. Conjunctivae mildly pale.
Chest/Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no dullness to percussion at the lung bases suggesting effusion.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; no peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Moderately distended. Flanks bulging with a positive fluid wave consistent with moderate-volume ascites. A firm, irregular, non-tender pelvic mass is palpable extending from the pelvis to approximately the level of the umbilicus. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly is appreciated. Bowel sounds present throughout. No abdominal guarding or rebound tenderness.
Pelvic examination: (performed) Vaginal vault unremarkable; cervix grossly normal; bimanual examination confirms a large, fixed, irregular pelvic mass occupying the cul-de-sac and bilateral adnexa, consistent with bilateral adnexal masses as seen on CT imaging. Rectovaginal examination: mass palpable in the cul-de-sac with extension to the posterior pelvic sidewall bilaterally; no obvious nodal deposits palpable in the rectovaginal septum.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3; no focal deficits.

---

LABORATORY DATA (REVIEWED):
CA-125: 3,847 U/mL (markedly elevated; normal <35 U/mL)
HE4: 412 pmol/L (markedly elevated; normal <70 pmol/L in premenopausal women)
CBC: WBC 8.2 x10^9/L, Hgb 11.1 g/dL (mild anemia, likely iron deficiency and/or chronic disease), platelets 347 x10^9/L
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 3.9 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.84 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2)
Liver function tests: AST 28 U/L, ALT 22 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, albumin 3.4 g/dL (mildly low, likely related to malnutrition and ascites)
Coagulation studies: PT/INR 1.1, aPTT 29 seconds
LDH: 198 U/L (within normal limits)
Type and screen: sent in anticipation of surgery

IMAGING DATA (REVIEWED):
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast: Large bilateral complex adnexal masses (right 7.2 x 6.1 x 5.8 cm, left 5.8 x 4.3 cm), moderate ascites, omental caking up to 11 cm, peritoneal implants largest 2.3 cm along the right paracolic gutter and small bowel mesentery, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusion, no pulmonary nodules, no parenchymal liver metastases, no suprarenal disease. Estimated PCI ~18. Disease radiographically amenable to primary surgical cytoreduction.

PATHOLOGY (REVIEWED):
Omental biopsy: High-grade serous carcinoma of Mullerian origin with characteristic IHC profile (WT-1+, PAX8+, p53 aberrant, CK7+, CK20-). Ki-67 78%. Peritoneal cytology positive.

GENETICS (REVIEWED):
Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant c.5266dupC (5382insC founder mutation). Tumor NGS with somatic LOH at BRCA1, high HRD score 68, gLOH 31%, TP53 p.R248W. MSS, TMB-low.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old premenopausal woman with newly diagnosed high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) of bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0), with extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis, ascites, and bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy. Her tumor carries the hallmarks of HGSC: WT-1/PAX8 positivity, p53 aberrant pattern, and an extremely elevated CA-125. She carries a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC), and her tumor demonstrates somatic LOH at the BRCA1 locus with a high HRD score — a highly favorable predictive biomarker for both platinum sensitivity and PARP inhibitor sensitivity.

1. SURGICAL PLANNING — PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS):
Based on the CT imaging, diagnostic laparoscopy findings, and her overall excellent functional status (ECOG 1), Ms. Osei-Bonsu is deemed a good candidate for primary debulking surgery with the goal of achieving complete gross resection (R0). The importance of achieving R0 resection — the single most important prognostic surgical variable in advanced HGSC — was discussed in detail. Planned surgical procedure includes exploratory laparotomy, total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (TAH-BSO), infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy (given the proximity of the omental and peritoneal disease), bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, and peritoneal implant resection. The possibility of upper abdominal surgery including diaphragmatic stripping was discussed. Based on current disease distribution, bowel resection does not appear necessary, though this cannot be fully excluded until exploration. The surgical complexity score is estimated as moderate-high, and the case will be approached with full colorectal surgery backup.

Risks discussed at length with patient and husband: bleeding, infection, wound complications, injury to bowel, bladder, and ureters, lymphocele formation, prolonged ileus, deep venous thrombosis and pulmonary embolism (VTE prophylaxis will be employed perioperatively and for 28 days postoperatively), need for ostomy (low but non-zero probability), ICU admission, and risks related to anesthesia. Expected hospital stay: 4-6 days. Patient verbalized understanding of risks and benefits.

2. ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY PLANNING:
Following surgery (anticipated 4-6 weeks for recovery), the patient will proceed with platinum-based adjuvant chemotherapy: carbo (AUC 5-6) plus Taxol (175 mg/m2) IV every 21 days for 6 cycles. This is the international standard of care for FIGO stage III HGSC following optimal debulking surgery, supported by GOG-0158 and ICON3 data, and consistent with current NCCN guidelines. The role of Avastin (Avastin) in the upfront setting was discussed (ICON7, GOG-0218 data); given the planned R0 resection (which confers a more favorable prognosis), the incremental benefit of bev was felt to be modest and bev was not planned to be added to the upfront regimen. Intraperitoneal chemotherapy (IP/IV) was also discussed; given contemporary data and patient preference for IV access, standard IV dosing was favored.

3. MAINTENANCE THERAPY PLANNING:
Given her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant with somatic LOH and the expectation that she will achieve a complete or near-complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy (given her HRD-high tumor biology), she is an excellent candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy following chemotherapy. Options include Lynparza (Lynparza) and Zejula (Zejula). Preliminary discussions were initiated; final decision will be deferred to medical oncology consultation and post-chemotherapy response assessment. The SOLO-1 trial demonstrated a significant improvement in progression-free survival with ola maintenance in germline BRCA1/2 patients achieving response to first-line platinum therapy; PRIMA also demonstrated benefit of nira in HRD-positive patients. Total planned maintenance duration is 3 years per current evidence.

4. GERMLINE BRCA1 IMPLICATIONS:
The significance of the germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC pathogenic variant was reviewed with the patient. She has an increased lifetime risk of breast cancer (approximately 65-72% by age 80) and of a second primary ovarian/fallopian tube malignancy (mitigated by current BSO). She was counseled that her first-degree relatives (daughter, son, sister) may be at 50% risk of carrying the same variant. Her daughter (age 22) and sister (age 44) were specifically discussed as candidates for genetic counseling and testing. Referral to our hereditary cancer program was placed. Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy has already been addressed by the current surgery; breast cancer surveillance and risk reduction will need to be coordinated with breast surgery and/or medical genetics following recovery.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is visibly distressed and tearful during this consultation, expressing fear about her diagnosis, anxiety regarding her children (particularly the implications of her BRCA1 status for her daughter), and concern about surgical recovery and chemotherapy side effects. She was reassured that the multidisciplinary team will support her throughout. Referral to oncology social work was placed. She was also provided information on the gynecologic oncology patient support group that meets biweekly. Her husband was present and engaged; he was included in all discussions. The importance of open communication with her support network was emphasized.

6. ADDITIONAL CONSIDERATIONS:
- Pre-operative anesthesia and cardiopulmonary evaluation to be completed prior to scheduling.
- Pre-operative bowel preparation will be discussed with the surgical team.
- The patient's mild anemia (Hgb 11.1 g/dL) was noted; iron supplementation already initiated. Type and screen sent. Perioperative transfusion threshold and potential for cell salvage discussed.
- VTE risk: elevated given malignancy, planned major abdominal surgery. Mechanical compression devices intraoperatively; enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneous daily to begin 12-24 hours postoperatively and to continue for 28 days post-discharge per GOG/ASCO VTE guidelines.
- Nutrition consult placed given mild hypoalbuminemia (3.4 g/dL) and 6 kg weight loss; prehabilitation nutritional optimization to be pursued in the brief preoperative window.
- Patient wig referral not yet placed at this visit; to be addressed at subsequent oncology visit.
- Fertility: the patient has completed childbearing (G2P2); bilateral oophorectomy required as part of the operation; premature surgical menopause will result and hormone replacement therapy was briefly discussed, though typically deferred in the immediate postoperative/chemotherapy period. Menopause management to be addressed in follow-up.

The case will be presented at the multidisciplinary gynecologic oncology tumor board for review prior to surgical scheduling. The patient and her husband expressed understanding of the overall plan and agreement to proceed. They were given written materials and the oncology nurse's contact information. Follow-up appointment scheduled in one week to confirm surgical date and complete pre-operative workup.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 75 minutes.

Electronically signed,
Attending Gynecologic Oncologist
